title: Home Page
text: The Home Page serves as the primary navigation hub for the Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC) Portal. This page provides quick access to the main functionalities of the portal, allowing users to search for BGCs, access API documentation, find user guides, and learn more about the project.

title: BGC Details
text: This section provides a detailed guide on how to explore the BGC details page within the Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC) Portal. Users can gain insights into the predicted BGC regions, understand various annotations, and download relevant data files.

title: Explore BGCs
text:
The Advanced Search bar allows you to perform fine-grained queries to explore BGC Data Portal. Below is a detailed explanation of each search field and examples of complex search queries to help you utilise this feature effectively.



BGC Class Name:
Enter one or more BGC class names (i.e., alkaloid, nrp, other, polyketide, ripp, saccharide, or terpene) separated by spaces or commas. The search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the specified classes (using an OR logic).
MGYB:
Specify a particular MGYB (BGC accession of portal) to directly retrieve data associated with that BGC.
Assembly Accession:
Input an ENA assembly accession to find predicted BGCs within that specific assembly.
Contig MGYC:
Provide a specific MGYC (contig accession) to locate BGCs within that contig.
Biome:
Enter terms related to the biome lineage (e.g., sediment, hydrothermal vent) to filter BGCs that originate from assemblies associated with those biomes.
BGC Completeness:
Select from the following options to refine your search based on the BGCs’ contig boundaries:

Full-Length BGC: The BGC is entirely within the contig, meaning both start and end positions are within the boundaries of the contig, indicating a complete prediction.
Single: Partial BGC (Single edge). The BGC extends to one end of the contig, suggesting that the prediction may be truncated at one side because the contig ends there.
Double: Partial BGC (Double edges). The BGC spans the entire length of the contig, indicating that the prediction is likely truncated on both sides, with both ends of the BGC coinciding with the ends of the contig.

Pfam:
Enter one or more Pfam accessions (e.g., PF00109, PF00501) separated by spaces or commas. You can define the strategy to search BGCs containing these Pfams:

OR: The resulting BGCs have at least one of the specified Pfams.
AND: The resulting BGCs must include all specified Pfams.

Pfam Strategy:
The Pfam Strategy field determines how the search will interpret multiple Pfam entries. You have two options:

OR Logic: When you select the OR strategy, the search will return BGCs that contain at least one of the Pfams you specified. This is useful when you are interested in BGCs that have any of a list of potential Pfams, allowing for a broader search. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and select OR, the search will return BGCs that contain either PF00109, PF00501, or both.
AND Logic: Selecting the AND strategy will narrow the search results to BGCs that must contain all the Pfams you listed. This is ideal when you are looking for BGCs with a specific combination of Pfams. For example, if you enter PF00109 PF00501 and choose AND, only those BGCs that contain both PF00109 and PF00501 will be returned.

Aggregate Strategy:
The Aggregate Strategy determines how BGC regions predicted by different detection tools are combined in the final results. This field is important when you want to either view individual predictions or identify consensus regions supported by multiple tools. There are three strategies to choose from:

Single:
This strategy displays the predictions from each tool separately, even if they overlap. When using this option, you might see multiple entries for the same region if different tools predicted similar but not identical regions. This strategy is useful for comparing how different tools view the same region but does not combine their outputs into a single prediction.
Union:
The Union strategy merges regions where at least one gene overlaps across predictions from different tools. The resulting region will be defined by the outermost start and end positions of these overlapping predictions. This strategy creates a broader region that represents all potential BGCs detected by at least one of the selected tools, providing a more inclusive view of the data. It is useful when you want to ensure that you capture all possible BGCs, even if only partially supported by some tools.
Intersection:
The Intersection strategy is the most stringent. It only includes regions where all the selected detection tools agree. The resulting BGC region will be the longest contiguous sequence that all tools have predicted as a BGC. This strategy is particularly useful when you want high-confidence predictions, as it guarantees that the region is supported by multiple independent tools, reducing the likelihood of false positives.

title: API Documentation
text:
An API (Application Programming Interface) allows different software applications to communicate with each other. In the context of the Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC) Portal, the API provides a programmatic way for users to access and interact with the portal’s data. This can be especially useful for automating workflows, integrating the portal’s data with other bioinformatics tools, or performing large-scale analyses without needing to use the web interface.



For detailed information on how to use the API, including available endpoints and request formats, please refer to the comprehensive API documentation available on the Portal’s Home page under the “API Access” section.

title: Introduction
text:
Biosynthetic gene clusters are important for understanding the production of natural products, including antibiotics and other bioactive compounds. The BGC Portal provides a resource for researchers who need access to predicted biosynthetic gene clusters from a wide range of metagenomic datasets from MGnify.
The Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC) Portal is a platform designed to facilitate the exploration, analysis, and retrieval of biosynthetic gene cluster predictions from metagenomic assemblies.
The portal integrates data from multiple prediction tools, enabling users to perform detailed searches, visualise results, and download data in various formats.

title: Getting Started
text:
The Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC) Portal is designed with three primary components that help users navigate and utilise its features effectively. Below is an overview of these components:


The Home Page is your entry point to the BGC Portal. Here, you can navigate to different sections of the site, perform a quick keyword search, and access key functionalities. The main features of the Home Page include:

Keyword Search: Located at the top of the page, this allows you to search the portal using specific keywords or accession numbers, retrieving relevant records from both documentation and data.
Access and Explore Options:

Search BGCs: Directs you to the Explore BGCs page for more detailed queries.
API Access: Provides documentation on how to use the API for programmatic access to BGC data.
User Documentation: Guides and examples to help you navigate and utilise the portal effectively.
About Us: Information about the project, including details on the EUREMAP initiative supporting marine bioprospecting research.





The Explore BGCs Page is where users can perform detailed searches and view the results. It includes:

Advanced Search Sidebar: This allows for complex queries using various criteria, including BGC class names, specific accessions, biome data, and more.
Results Table: Displays the outcomes of your search in a paginated format, with options to download the data in TSV or Bash script formats. The table includes key details such as BGC accession, assembly accession, contig information, and detector names.




The BGC Details Page provides an in-depth view of a specific biosynthetic gene cluster. Key features include:

Aggregated Region Basic Information: Displays essential details such as assembly accession, contig information, biome data, and the BGC classes predicted.
BGC Plot: A visual representation of the BGC, including coding regions, Pfam annotations, and predictions from various detectors.
CDS Data: Detailed information about coding sequences, including protein sequences, Pfam descriptions, and links to external resources.
Download Options: Allows you to download data related to the BGC in various formats, including GeneBank, JSON, FASTA, and GFF3.

title: Getting Started
text:
Glossary of Terms
Below are definitions of key terms and concepts used in the BGC Portal:

Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC): A group of genes that collectively are predicted to produce a secondary metabolite by a specific BGC detector, often with ecological or pharmaceutical significance.
Aggregated Region: In the context of the BGC Portal, an aggregated region refers to a genomic region that is identified as a BGC through the combined predictions of multiple BGC detection tools. The aggregation process involves comparing the predictions from different detectors and merging them based on specific strategies (i.e., single, union, intersection). The resulting aggregated region represents the consensus prediction, which may span across overlapping regions identified by the different tools, ensuring a comprehensive and reliable identification of the BGC.
MGYB: A specific accession number related to each BGC predictions within the portal.
Aggregate Strategy: The method used to combine predictions from multiple BGC detectors. This can be done using single, union, or intersection strategies to determine the final predicted BGC regions.

For more detailed instructions, please refer to the specific sections of the documentation.

title: Home Page
text:
Home Page Features

Keyword Search
At the top of every page, you will find the Keyword Search bar. This feature allows you to quickly perform a basic search across the BGC Portal data and documents using specific keywords or accessions. This tool is designed to provide a fast and straightforward way to retrieve data without requiring advanced search criteria.


Access and Explore Options
The Home Page offers several options to access and explore metagenomic BGC data:
Explore BGCs gives access to more detailed searches using advanced criteria. This feature is particularly useful for users who need to perform complex queries or refine their search results based on specific parameters. Also provides basic statistics on the data portal alongside the detailed results of their searches.
API Access section provides documentation for users who wish to programmatically interact with the BGC Portal. Review the documentation, which includes endpoint descriptions and parameter details.
User Documentation section is where you will find guides on how to use the BGC Portal.
About Us section provides background information on the BGC Portal and the team behind its development.
This page is access point to exploring and utilising the BGC Portal.

title: BGC Details
text:
Detailed View of Aggregated Regions

Basic Information
At the top of the BGC details page, users will find essential information about the aggregated region. This includes:

Assembly Accession: The ENA (European Nucleotide Archive) assembly accession associated with the predicted BGC region. This is a unique identifier for the assembly from which the BGC was predicted. The assembly accession is linked to the MGnify assembly page, where users can explore the assembly in more detail.
Contig Information: The contig accession (MGYC) where the BGC was predicted. The contig is a segment of the assembly, and the accession is linked to the MGnify contig viewer, allowing further exploration of the contig.
Biome Data: Information about the biome from which the assembly originates. Biome data provides context on the environmental source of the DNA sequence, which can be important for understanding the ecological relevance of the BGC.
Prediction Details: This includes the start and end positions of the aggregated region within the contig, the BGC detectors involved in the prediction, and the BGC classes predicted within the region. This data helps users understand the scope and source of the BGC prediction.

title: BGC Details
text:
BGC Plot
The BGC plot is a visual representation of the predicted BGC within the contig. It includes several layers of information:

Coding Regions (CDS): Displayed as white arrows with black edges, each arrow represents a coding sequence within the contig. The direction of the arrow indicates the strand on which the gene is located. Users can click on these arrows to reveal additional CDS data, including protein sequence and Pfam annotations.
Pfam annotations: Pfam domains within the CDS are represented by colored arrows inside the white CDS arrows. The color corresponds to the Gene Ontology (GO) slim molecular function associated with that Pfam. Clicking reveals additional CDS data, including links to the Pfan entries in InterPro.
BGC predictions: Horizontal colored bars at the bottom of the plot represent BGC predictions by individual detection tools.
Aggregated region: An unfilled horizontal bar at the bottom of the plot represents the aggregated BGC region. This region encompasses the consensus prediction from all specified detectors, and its coordinates are reflected in the results table.

title: BGC Details
text:
CDS Data
When users click on a coding sequence (CDS) in the plot, detailed information about the CDS is displayed:

Protein Sequence: The amino acid sequence of the protein encoded by the CDS. This sequence is crucial for further functional analysis or comparisons with other sequences.
Pfam Details: Information about the Pfam domain(s) within the CDS, including descriptions and associated GO terms. This helps in understanding the potential function of the protein.
Representative Protein: A link to the representative protein in the MGnify proteins database. This link provides additional information about the protein, including its structure, biome distribution, and other relevant annotations.

title: BGC Details
text:
Download Options
The BGC details page offers download options for users who wish to retrieve data for further analysis:

GeneBank: This file contains the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region, along with annotated features such as predicted genes, translation sequences, Pfam domains, and BGC region information. The GeneBank format is widely used for storing detailed sequence data, and it is compatible with various bioinformatics tools.
JSON: The JSON file is formatted according to antiSMASH specifications and can be used directly within the antiSMASH ecosystem. This file is useful for users who want to perform further analysis using antiSMASH tools or integrate the data into their existing antiSMASH workflows.
FASTA: This file provides the nucleotide sequence of the aggregated BGC region in FASTA format. FASTA is a simple, widely-used format for representing nucleotide sequences, making it easy to use with various sequence analysis tools.
GFF3: The GFF3 file contains the annotations of the aggregated BGC region and its constituent CDSs. The GFF3 format is commonly used for representing gene features and is compatible with many genome browsers and annotation tools.

These download options allow users to take the predicted BGC data into their preferred analysis pipelines, ensuring flexibility and compatibility with different bioinformatics platforms.

title: Explore BGCs
text:
Results Table
After conducting a search, the results will be displayed in a paginated table. Below are key components of the results summary and table, along with instructions for downloading and interpreting the data.

Results Summary

Total Number of Aggregated Regions

Class Distribution plot
BGC Completeness Distribution plot



Explanation of Table Columns

MGYB:
The BGC accession (or aggregated accession) for the resulting region.
Assembly Accession:
The ENA assembly accession number where the BGC was predicted.
Contig MGYC:
The accession of the contig containing the BGC.
Start Position:
The start coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
End Position:
The end coordinate of the BGC within the contig.
Detector Names:
Lists the BGC detectors that contributed to the aggregated prediction.
Class Names:
The predicted BGC classes within the aggregated region.



Downloading Search Results
You can download the search results as a TSV file, which can be used in conjunction with the API to retrieve the data locally.
This information will help you explore, interpret, and utilise the data from the BGC Portal effectively.
